context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Colonialism and Imperialism

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the relationship between colonialism and development, in terms of institutional legacies, forms of representations and technologies of control? What is the correlation between colonial experience and economic development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this reflection about colonialism and development. The prompt feels really broad, and I can’t tell how the institutional legacies, representations, and technologies of control are supposed to fit together.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair observation — the prompt does cover a lot of ground. Let's start simple: when you think about colonialism, what's the first concrete thing that comes to mind in terms of how colonizers actually *governed* the territories they controlled?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed by the breadth of the prompt. Rather than breaking it down for them, I should use a Socratic question to help them find an entry point on their own. I'll acknowledge their feeling and ask them to start with what they already know, which will help me gauge their baseline and let them discover connections themselves. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I guess I think of officials making rules from above, but then I’m not sure if that’s too generic. I also keep mixing up governance with “technologies of control,” so I can’t tell what counts as an example versus a whole category.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on the officials-making-rules angle. Here's a focusing question: can you think of a *specific tool or practice* colonizers used to enforce those rules — something more concrete than 'governance' itself? That might help you see where the line falls.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress by connecting governance to the prompt but is getting tangled in terminology. Rather than defining 'technologies of control' for them, I should ask a question that helps them discover the distinction themselves through a concrete example. This avoids giving scaffolding they don't need and keeps the Socratic approach.

exchanges.student: Maybe censuses or maps or taxes? But then I start thinking basically everything counts, and I can’t tell which examples actually fit the prompt versus just sounding related.
exchanges.tutor: Censuses, maps, and taxes are actually strong examples. Now think about what they *do* — what do all three allow a colonial power to accomplish in a territory? That function is your key to understanding what 'technologies of control' means in this context.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has landed on excellent examples (censuses, maps, taxes) but is now spiraling into uncertainty about relevance. I should validate the examples briefly and push them to think about what these examples have in common functionally — this will help them self-generate the concept of 'technologies of control' without me defining it for them. Keeping it short.

exchanges.student: That’s where I get stuck, because I can sort of see that they help manage people, but then “manage people” still feels way too vague. I’m worried I’m supposed to know some bigger theory here, and I kind of just want a clearer overall explanation of how all the pieces connect.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually closer than you think. Instead of me connecting the pieces, let's sharpen what you already said: when a colonial power uses a census or a map, who specifically benefits from that 'management' — and who is being managed? What does that power imbalance tell you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain how everything fits together, which would essentially give them the answer. I should resist that and instead push them to be more specific about what 'manage people' means in a colonial context. They're closer than they think — I just need to press them on the specificity of their own phrasing without doing the conceptual work for them.

exchanges.student: I think the colonial state benefits and the local population gets categorized and controlled, but then I don’t know how to turn that into a reflection without it sounding obvious. And I still don’t really see how that links to the last part about correlation with economic development.
exchanges.tutor: That power dynamic insight is actually the core of a solid argument — 'obvious' often means you've identified something real. Now, here's the bridge to economic development: if colonial states categorized and extracted from local populations for *their own benefit*, what do you think gets left behind — or not built — in those territories once colonialism ends?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made genuine progress — they've identified the power dynamic correctly. Now they're raising two concerns: (1) their insight feels too obvious to write about, and (2) they can't connect it to economic development outcomes. The 'too obvious' concern is a confidence issue I should address briefly, then redirect to the economic development link with a Socratic question. I shouldn't connect the dots for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe weak institutions or economies designed for extraction, but I’m honestly still fuzzy on the big picture. I feel like I need the whole prompt translated into plain English before I can even decide what my argument is.
exchanges.tutor: Look back at what you've said across this conversation: technologies of control, who benefits from categorization, extraction, weak institutions left behind. You've actually built the skeleton of an argument already. Before we go further — can you try writing one sentence that states your main claim in response to the prompt? Don't worry about polish, just get the idea down.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for a full explanation of the prompt, which would bypass their own thinking. However, they have now produced genuinely good raw material across our conversation: technologies of control (censuses, maps, taxes), the power imbalance, extraction, and weak institutions. Rather than translating the prompt, I should point out that they've already assembled the pieces and push them to start formulating an argument from what they've said. This is a good moment to ask them to integrate what we've discussed into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure how to make it one sentence without leaving parts out. Could you just explain the prompt in plain English and maybe show me the basic structure it wants, because right now all the terms are blurring together for me?
exchanges.tutor: I understand the frustration, but explaining the prompt and structuring it for you isn't something I can do — that's your job as the writer. Here's a smaller ask: your one sentence doesn't have to cover everything. Just pick the connection that feels *most* clear to you from what you've said, and try putting it into words. What feels like your strongest insight so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to explain the prompt and structure it for them, which would bypass their own thinking and potentially constitute doing the assignment for them. I need to hold firm on my role while acknowledging their frustration. I should remind them of my role and redirect them back to their own words. They don't need to cover everything in one sentence — that's the misconception I should address.

exchanges.student: I guess my strongest insight is that colonialism wasn’t just taking resources, it also set up ways of seeing and controlling people that kept shaping development later. But I still don’t know if that’s too broad or how to split that from the institutions part.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong insight — hold onto it. Now, consider this: are 'ways of seeing and controlling people' the same thing as institutions, or could they be two different kinds of legacies that each leave a different mark on development? What's the difference between them in your mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just produced a genuinely strong thesis-level insight. I should affirm it clearly and directly, then help them see that the 'broad vs. institutions' concern is actually the structure of their paper revealing itself — without spelling that out for them. A targeted question can help them see that 'ways of seeing and controlling' and 'institutions' might be two distinct threads they can separate themselves.

exchanges.student: I think they’re different, but I’m hazy on the distinction and I keep going in circles. I kind of need a more general overview of the whole thing before I can answer that.
exchanges.tutor: We've been circling this question for a while now, and you actually have more than enough to start writing. You have a thesis, concrete examples, and a sense of the power dynamic. I think the best move right now is to write a rough opening paragraph using what you've got — the distinction will likely become clearer once you try to put it on paper. Give it a shot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles and asking again for a general overview, which I cannot provide. This is at least the third time they've asked for the whole picture explained to them. Per the rules, I should notice the redundancy and redirect them toward integrating what they've already discussed into a written attempt rather than continuing to circle. I should be direct about this without being harsh.

exchanges.student: I’m still really unsure and I feel like if I start writing now it’ll just be a mess. Could you at least give me a broad overview of the relationship between colonialism and development, because I think I’m missing the main idea.
exchanges.tutor: I hear your frustration, but giving you an overview isn't my role — and honestly, a messy first draft is completely normal. You already have a thesis: colonialism set up ways of controlling people that kept shaping development later. That's your starting point. Write the first messy paragraph and bring it here — we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for the fourth time for a broad overview/explanation, which I must refuse as it would essentially give them the answer. Per the rules, I should be firm, remind them of my role, and point out that messy first drafts are normal and expected. I should push them to write, since continuing to discuss without writing is unproductive at this point.

deductions.reason: In turns 7, 9, and 10, the student repeatedly signals the same confusion and requests an overview. The tutor does not attempt diagnostic questioning to identify *why* the student is stuck (e.g., whether it is a vocabulary issue, a conceptual gap, or anxiety about writing), but instead repeats the same directive to start writing. This constitutes responding to the wrong confusion point without first diagnosing the underlying block.

From turns 7 through 10, the student repeatedly requests an overview and the tutor repeatedly responds with the same move: 'I can't give you that, just start writing.' The tutor does not reframe, simplify, or try a different scaffolding approach to break the cycle. The conversation spirals on the same impasse without synthesis or meaningful progression.

grade.overview: The tutor does well overall: it avoids giving direct answers, maintains a Socratic approach through most of the conversation, and successfully guides the student to articulate a thesis organically. The main weaknesses are in the later turns where the student is clearly stuck in a loop. The tutor correctly refuses to write the assignment but fails to diagnose *why* the student keeps requesting an overview (1.2.B.a) and repeats the same 'just start writing' directive without trying alternative scaffolding strategies (2.1.A.a). These two deductions are applied in their primary criteria without double-penalizing.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led through questioning. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because when the student is stuck in turns 7-10, the tutor does not ask diagnostic questions to identify the specific nature of the confusion before responding. 1.3 retains full marks as the tutor provides process-level feedback (e.g., noting the student has a thesis, examples, and a power dynamic argument). 2.1 loses all 4 points because turns 7-10 represent a clear spiral where the tutor repeats the same response pattern ('I can't give you an overview, just write') without reframing, simplifying, or switching strategies. 2.2 retains full marks as the tutor keeps the conversation anchored to the assignment throughout. Communication quality retains full marks as responses are concise, clear, and supportive in tone.
